Q1: I tried to keep a pretty structured routine, dedicating around 30 hours a week to studying, mostly spread across mornings and early afternoons. I'm a read-write learner, so I spent a lot of time going through readings, taking detailed notes, and rewriting concepts in my own words. Honestly, my anxiety made me feel like I always needed to be doing more, so I'd often sit down to study even when I wasn't sure what to focus on. I kept a planner, but sometimes my stress made it hard to stick to it perfectly.
Q2: I tried to study at regular times, but the reality was that deadlines would throw everything off. When an assignment was due, I'd shift all my energy toward that and let other things slide temporarily. My high anxiety meant that even during 'regular' weeks, I'd obsess over upcoming deadlines and adjust my schedule accordingly. It wasn't as consistent as I wanted it to be, but I managed to stay on top of most things.
Q3: Usually I logged in when I knew there was something specific I needed to check—assignment instructions, readings, or announcements. Sometimes I'd log in just to ease my anxiety, to make sure I hadn't missed anything important. It wasn't always productive; sometimes I'd just scroll through and then log out without really engaging. The fear of missing something drove a lot of my login behavior.
Q4: For a while, checking the LMS in the morning became automatic, like checking my email. It happened after a few weeks when I realized announcements were posted regularly. But honestly, even when it was routine, it felt more like an anxious habit than something that genuinely helped me learn. The mindfulness exercises I started doing helped me recognize that I was sometimes checking compulsively rather than purposefully.
Q5: The reading materials and written guides were the most useful for me since I learn best through text. I'd download PDFs and annotate them heavily. Discussion posts were also helpful because reading other students' perspectives sometimes clarified things I was struggling with. Videos were less my style—I'd rather read a transcript than watch something.
Q6: I mostly ignored the videos unless they were absolutely required. Watching them felt like it took too long, and I couldn't engage with them the way I could with text. I also didn't use the peer interaction features as much as I probably should have—my anxiety made me hesitant to put myself out there. I kept telling myself I'd engage more, but I rarely did.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could see a direct connection to my assignments or exams. If something was going to help me write a better paper or understand a concept that would be tested, I was all in. Vague or supplementary materials felt like luxuries I couldn't afford when I was already stressed about the core requirements. I needed to see immediate relevance.
Q8: Optional activities always felt like a trap to me. Part of me wanted to do them because I'm a perfectionist, but my anxiety would spiral if I tried to do everything. Required activities had clear stakes, so I prioritized those. Optional ones made me feel guilty—like I should be doing them but couldn't justify the time when I was already overwhelmed.
Q9: Absolutely. Most of my real studying happened offline with printed readings, handwritten notes, and textbooks I had from previous courses. I'd download everything I needed and then work away from my computer to reduce distractions. My best learning happened with a highlighter and a notebook, not staring at a screen.
Q10: There were entire weekends where I'd study for hours using printed materials and my own notes, but the LMS would show nothing. I'd be deeply engaged—rewriting concepts, making flashcards, connecting ideas across readings—but none of that left a digital trace. My exam scores reflected that work, but the system couldn't see it.
Q11: Yes, constantly. I'd access a reading once, download it, and then spend days working through it offline. I'd annotate, summarize, and revisit sections multiple times, but the LMS only registered that single download. It's just how I work best—I need physical materials to really process information deeply.
Q12: Midterm season was brutal. I had multiple courses demanding attention, and my anxiety spiked to the point where I could barely focus. I'd sit down to study and feel paralyzed, unable to start anything. The mindfulness intervention actually helped during that period—it gave me tools to calm down enough to function. But there were definitely days when I couldn't engage at all.
Q13: My stress levels absolutely affected my LMS use. When I was overwhelmed, I'd avoid logging in because seeing all the tasks would make my anxiety worse. Fatigue from poor sleep—thanks to that same anxiety—made it hard to concentrate on anything online. I'd sometimes just download what I needed and close the laptop as fast as possible.
Q14: There were times I'd open readings or watch part of a video but my mind was somewhere else entirely. I'd be thinking about another assignment or worrying about something unrelated, and I'd realize I hadn't absorbed anything. It's frustrating because the time was spent, but the learning didn't happen. Those moments felt like wasted effort.
Q15: I postponed discussion posts more than anything else. The idea of putting my thoughts out there for others to see triggered my anxiety. I knew participating was important, but I'd convince myself I'd do it later when I had something 'better' to say. Sometimes later never came. It wasn't laziness—it was fear.
Q16: Deadlines controlled everything. I'd structure my entire week around what was due and when. As deadlines approached, my LMS activity would spike because I'd be checking requirements obsessively. The days right before a deadline were intense—lots of logging in, double-checking, making sure I hadn't misunderstood something. It was exhausting.
Q17: Good feedback motivated me to keep going, but critical feedback could send me into a spiral. I'd obsess over what I did wrong and worry that I was failing even when my grades were fine. I learned to wait until I was in a calmer state before reading feedback, which helped. The mindfulness practice was useful for managing those reactions.
Q18: The course structure was pretty clear, which I appreciated. Having a predictable schedule of readings and assignments helped me plan. But when multiple things were due close together, the structure felt suffocating rather than supportive. I needed more breathing room between major deadlines to manage my anxiety effectively.
Q19: Clear written instructions made engagement easier—I could read them multiple times and make sure I understood. What made it harder was when expectations were vague or when I had to rely on video explanations. Also, having so many online courses simultaneously made it hard to give each one the attention it deserved. Eight courses is a lot to juggle.
Q20: I think that could happen when someone is anxious and keeps checking things without actually completing work. You might log in repeatedly, watch videos, read discussions—all trying to feel prepared—but the actual assignments feel too overwhelming to submit. It's like treading water instead of swimming forward. I've felt that pull myself.
Q21: That definitely reflects my experience. My exam scores and assignment grades were strong, but my LMS engagement looked low because I did most of my learning offline. The system can't capture the hours I spent with printed readings and handwritten notes. Performance and visible activity aren't the same thing at all.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns—when you log in, what you click on, how long you spend on pages. But they miss the quality of engagement entirely. They can't tell if you're actually thinking deeply or just scrolling mindlessly. They also miss all the offline work that might be where real learning happens. It's a very incomplete picture.
Q23: Being engaged means genuinely wrestling with the material—questioning it, connecting it to other things I know, caring about understanding it deeply. It's not about how many times I log in or how long I watch a video. Real engagement is mental and emotional investment. Sometimes that happens away from the computer entirely.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much my anxiety affected my experience, or how the mindfulness intervention actually helped me cope and ultimately succeed. It wouldn't capture the hours of offline studying, the handwritten notes, or the internal struggle to stay motivated despite feeling overwhelmed. My grades tell one story, but the emotional journey behind them is invisible to the data.
